data.title: Pam Bondi fired despite commitment to Trump agenda: Loyalty not enough!
data.description:
Yesterday we found out that loyalty can get you the job with President Trump, but it certainly won’t help you keep it.  

Pam Bondi’s rise to attorney general was built on exactly that — loyalty to Donald Trump and a willingness to carry out his agenda. And by many accounts, she did just that. She reshaped the Justice Department, broke with long-standing norms of independence, and leaned into politically charged investigations targeting Trump’s rivals.  

But in the end, that just wasn’t enough.  

The president says Bondi is “transitioning” to the private sector, praising her record and offering a polished exit. But behind the scenes, the story is far less flattering. Sources say Trump was frustrated, especially that Bondi hadn’t gone far enough or successfully enough in prosecuting his political opponents.  

What?!  

Because from my purview, some of her cases didn’t fail for lack of effort — they failed because they were weak.  

Indictments against figures like James Comey and Letitia James were thrown out by judges. Prosecutors raised concerns about other investigations, including whether there was even a viable case to bring. In other words, the legal system — imperfect as it is — still has guardrails.  

So Bondi found herself in an impossible position: pushed to pursue retribution, but constrained by the reality of what could actually hold up in court.  

That’s not to say her tenure was without controversy. The handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files became a major credibility issue, especially after she claimed during an interview that a client list was “sitting on my desk right now to review,” only for the department to later say no such list existed. That moment fueled bipartisan frustration and raised serious questions about transparency.

Still, if the expectation was delivering political victories through prosecutions, the system pushed back, and that appears to have sealed her fate.  

There’s also a broader pattern here. Bondi is now the second Cabinet official removed in recent weeks. And like others before her, she learned a hard lesson about serving in Trump’s orbit: performance is measured differently, and loyalty has an expiration date.  

What’s especially striking is that Bondi didn’t distance herself from the White House — she aligned with it. She helped execute a vision of a Justice Department that critics say became deeply politicized. And yet, she was still shown the door.  

So in the end, this wasn’t about independence. It was about results. And when those results didn’t come, whether because of legal limits, institutional checks or flawed cases, loyalty alone couldn’t save her.  

Pam Bondi’s dismissal underscores a reality that has defined this administration time and again: in Trump’s Washington, loyalty may be required — but it’s never enough. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Beyond 'all' and 'best': A common-sense energy strategy for national security
data.description:
Rising tensions in the Middle East and renewed volatility in global oil markets have brought energy security back to the forefront of the national conversation. As crude prices climb amid instability involving Iran and key shipping routes, President Trump pointed out that short-term price spikes will accompany broader strategic efforts to preserve stability, and reaffirmed that prices will “drop like a rock” soon. Moments like this underscore why energy policy must be grounded in resilience and long-term supply security.

For more than a decade, America’s energy debate has been shaped by ambitious climate goals and rapid technological change rather practical solutions to real issues. Affordability and national security have often been treated as secondary concerns, especially as policymakers pursued aggressive timelines to shrink domestic fossil fuel production. Today, surging electricity demand, intensifying geopolitical tensions, and the race for artificial intelligence dominance are reinforcing the importance of a reliable and resilient energy foundation. The U.S. cannot afford to retire dependable energy sources faster than it can replace them.

This moment has sharpened the divide between two competing energy frameworks — “all of the above” and “best of the above.” Some leaders on the left have gravitated toward an “all of the above” posture, temporarily supporting all energy sources in hopes of keeping costs down while meeting rising demand. Others, particularly on the right, have advanced a “best of the above” philosophy. As Department of Energy Undersecretary nominee Kyle Haustveit told a Senate committee, “Not all energy is created equally, and not all energy types should be deployed across every region of the U.S. and the world.” In practice, that stance has been interpreted as excluding specific technologies, such as wind, from consideration.

America doesn’t need energy approaches based on catchy slogans. We need energy common sense: expanding baseload capacity, prioritizing reliability, and tailoring deployment where each technology makes the most sense for long-term security. Reliable baseload generation remains the backbone of grid stability.

A commonsense energy approach not only taps into each energy source strategically but prioritizes energy addition over energy transition. This approach solves for the political trend of revisiting previously granted permits or reconsidering federally-backed financing for already approved projects in the name of transitioning away from energy sources deemed politically unfavorable. Normalizing the revocation of fully permitted projects establishes a dangerous precedent which destabilizes energy investment and security.

The international market underscores the stakes — Britain’s post-Ukraine energy crisis illustrated the real-world consequences of supply constraints and price spikes. Millions struggled to heat their homes and energy poverty surged highlighting how energy transitions that outpace reliable replacement carry tangible costs. The Iranian conflict could have similar impacts on the global community, and America’s infrastructure will be put to the test.

The rapid expansion of AI, cloud computing and data centers is increasing electricity consumption across the country. Neither constraining supply while demand accelerates nor launching everything, everywhere at once are serious governing strategies. Both risk destabilizing regional grids and amplifying price volatility, while contradicting the common-sense approach the White House set forth.

All energy source carries tradeoffs. Coal provides reliable, dispatchable power and sustains local economies, but it remains the most carbon-intensive of the major fuels and is poorly suited for dense urban environments. Solar is modular and increasingly affordable, yet intermittent and often mismatched with constant industrial loads. Natural gas can scale quickly and affordably, but cannot alone guarantee long-term grid stability. Wind expands capacity in offshore and rural regions, though it often faces integration costs and local siting opposition.

Energy policy should be guided not by ideology, but by reliability, affordability, scalability, and strategic resilience. At a moment when global instability can ripple through energy markets overnight, the U.S. cannot afford wishful thinking or fragmented approaches. Energy common sense is not a compromise — it is a strategy. It secures supply, strengthens the grid, and positions America to lead in an increasingly competitive and energy-hungry world.

The question is no longer whether we can afford to embrace it, but whether we can afford not to.

Guy Caruso is a former administrator for the Energy Information Administration and strategic advisor for GAIN.

data.title: International law experts: US strikes on Iran may have amounted to war crimes
data.description:
Over 100 international law experts in the U.S. signed an open letter Thursday, warning that American strikes in Iran could be deemed as war crimes. 

“We are seriously concerned about strikes that have hit schools, health facilities, and homes.” the letter reads.

It called out specifically the missile strike on the all-girls Iranian Shajarah Tayyebeh elementary school. There is an ongoing military investigation that preliminarly determined responsibility was on the U.S.

“The strike likely violates international humanitarian law, and if evidence is found that those responsible were reckless, it could also be a war crime,” the letter reads.

The letter comes after President Trump this week reiterated threats to strike Iran’s energy infrastructure if it refuses to agree to a peace deal in the coming weeks. The joint U.S.-Israeli military operation in the region is nearing the end of its fifth week.

“I can say tonight we are on track to complete all of America’s military objectives shortly, very shortly,” Trump said in a prime-time address on Wednesday. “We’re going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks; we’re going to bring them back to the stone ages where they belong. In the meantime, discussions are ongoing.”

The letter is signed by senior professors, former government legal advisers, leaders of prominent international law associations and military law experts, including from schools like ⁠Harvard, Yale, Stanford and the University of California.

It cited a mid-March comment from Trump indicating the U.S. may conduct strikes on Iran “just for fun” and that “I don’t need international law.” 

The letter also cited comments from Pentagon ​chief Pete Hegseth from early March in which he said the U.S. does not fight with “stupid ​rules of engagement.”

“Public statements by senior officials indicate an alarming disrespect for the rules of international humanitarian law accepted by states, and which protect both civilians and members of the armed forces,” the letter read. 

U.S. Central Command (Centcom) spokesperson Navy Capt. Tim Hawkins said Thursday, “U.S. forces do not target civilians, unlike the Iranian regime which has attacked civilian locations in neighboring countries more than 300 times.”

Hawkins made the statement in response to Iran’s Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baqaei who said hours earlier that the U.S. has a new precision strike missile and used one to kill the students in Shajarah Tayyebeh elementary school on Feb. 28, the first day of strikes carried out by the U.S. and Israel.

“The attack killed 21 young boys and girls,” Baqaei wrote. “The missile explodes above its target, dispersing thousands of lethal fragments (tungsten pellets) to maximize human casualties. This is a despicable war crime.”

The open letter urged the U.S. government and its allies to respect international humanitarian law. It also told the U.S. to uphold the United Nations Charter and to publicly make clear U.S. commitment to and respect for norms of international law.

data.title: Couric: Trump mail-in voting order, national voting list part of effort to 'scare election workers'
data.description:
Journalist Katie Couric on Thursday said President Trump’s latest attacks on mail-in voting and executive order to establish a national voting list are part of a “real effort” to interfere in U.S. elections and “scare election workers.”

Brennan Center for Justice director Sean Morales-Doyle told Couric on the latest episode of her podcast “Breaking News” that Trump’s actions and remarks over mail-in voting are outside of the president’s legal authority.

What is implied by Trump’s actions is that if election officials allow anyone who is not on a national list of who can legally vote, “then we’re coming for you, and you can worry about being criminally prosecuted,” Morales-Doyle said.

“So, it’s multi-faceted,” Couric replied. “It’s really also designed to intimidate and scare election workers.”

Earlier in the interview, Couric called Trump’s actions on mail-in voting sporadic but “seem to indicate a real effort to, excuse my French, but f— with our elections.”

“And I can say that on Substack, not on network news,” she added, prompting her and Morales-Doyle to laugh.

Morales-Doyle explained how Trump’s executive order directs the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) “to create a list of every single citizen that lives in every single state.” The second directive from the order calls for the U.S. Postal Service to only send ballots to citizens on the state’s approved mail-in ballot list. These ballots will now be in secured envelopes with barcodes for tracking.

“The president tried issuing an executive order to interfere in our elections, rewrite the rules, exactly a year ago, and that one was focused on requiring people to show a passport to vote and that kind of thing to register to vote, right?” Morales-Doyle said. “We sued over that one within days… pretty soon thereafter, all of us won and that order never went into effect because we were able to get injunctions before it ever went into effect.”

Couric said the administration’s “drip, drip, drip” actions are “really designed to do something on a macro level, which is to create a lot of distrust.”

Trump has long criticized mail-in voting, claiming that it contributes to voter fraud despite little evidence to support that claim. He has accused undocumented immigrants of voting overwhelmingly for Democrats and, on Tuesday, suggested he won three presidential elections “convincingly” despite losing to former President Biden in 2020.

Recently, he has ramped up his rhetoric against mail-in voting with the November midterm elections several months away.

The president on Thursday called mail-in voting “cheating” as he signed the executive order to create the list of eligible voters, even though he mailed his ballot for a special election in Florida last week.

Trump has also called for elections to be “nationalized” and has made the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act, a Republican-backed bill that includes a requirement that U.S. citizens show proof of citizenship to register to vote and provide an ID before casting their ballot, his top legislative priority.

Democratic lawmakers have warned that Trump will attempt to interfere with the November midterms. Trump critics and other observers have emphasized rising gas prices, the affordability issue, growing disapproval over the U.S. conflict in Iran and the president’s own approval ratings as reasons for why Republicans could lose control of Congress.

data.title: Austria bans US military plans from its airspace
data.description:
Austria is rejecting a request from the U.S. to use its airspace for military flights related to the war against Iran, with the Austrian government reaffirming its neutral stance and separating itself from President Trump’s war. 

“We want nothing to do with Trump’s policy of chaos and his war, which will bring us the next energy crisis,” Austria’s Vice Chancellor Andi Babler wrote in a post on the social media site X. 

“Neutrality is a precious asset in our country. No to war.”

A spokesperson for Austria’s Defense Ministry told Reuters that Austria rejected several U.S. requests to allow military-related flyovers of its airspace.

All ​U.S. flyover requests of a military ‌nature ⁠relating to the conflict in Iran had been rejected, the spokesperson told Reuters. Austria ​applies the ​same ⁠principle to other countries that are engaged ​in military conflict, the ​spokesperson ⁠added.

Austria is not a member of the NATO alliance and has a long-standing policy of neutrality. 

Still, Austria’s rebuff of U.S. military assistance requests is notable coming as other European nations have rejected becoming a party to Trump’s war, which the U.S. launched jointly with Israel at the end of February. 

Italy and Spain have also denied the U.S. access to its airspace related to the American military.

data.title: Beware Dr. Chatbot: Privacy laws don't protect health care data from AI
data.description:
Americans are increasingly turning to artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT for something deeply personal: their health. They are asking about symptoms, medications, mental health struggles and even sharing lab results and imaging reports. 

For many, AI has quietly become the first stop before seeing a physician — faster than a clinic visit, more accessible than a specialist and seemingly more private than discussing sensitive concerns with another human. A January report from OpenAI says that among its 800 million active ChatGPT users, one in four enters a query about health care weekly. Over 40 million per day ask ChatGPT health care-related questions. 

But that sense of privacy is, in many cases, an illusion. 

Most Americans assume that if they are discussing health, their information is protected under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act. That assumption is dangerously outdated. HIPAA applies to doctors, hospitals and insurers — not to consumer AI platforms. 

When patients share medical concerns with ChatGPT, they are not speaking to physicians bound by confidentiality. They are interacting with a technology platform governed primarily by its own privacy policies. The result is a widening gap between perceived privacy and actual legal protection — one that millions of Americans are unknowingly stepping into every day. 

What makes this moment different from the early days of internet search is not just scale, but depth. Users are no longer typing a few keywords into Google. They are providing rich, narrative health histories, complete with timelines, medications, family history and emotional context. In effect, they are creating something that begins to resemble a medical record — except it exists entirely outside the health care system.  

Health data is among the most valuable categories of personal information because it predicts not only current conditions but future risk, cost and longevity. And increasingly, that data is being generated in places where traditional safeguards do not apply. 

At the same time, a parallel transformation is unfolding inside the exam room. Ambient AI scribes, including platforms developed by Suki and Optum, are now listening to doctor-patient conversations, transcribing them and converting them into structured clinical documentation. 

These tools are often introduced as a solution to physician burnout, and they do reduce the burden of documentation. But they also fundamentally change the nature of the medical record. Instead of a physician summarizing a visit,entire conversations are captured, processed and stored, often by third-party systems. This shift has implications far beyond efficiency. When every word in the exam room becomes data, documentation begins to blur into optimization.  

Companies like Optum are already integrating these systems into broader analytics and revenue cycle workflows, linking what is said during a clinical encounter to coding, billing and payer decision-making. What was once a private conversation between doctor and patient is increasingly part of a larger financial and actuarial pipeline. The question is no longer just how care is delivered, but how it is recorded, interpreted and ultimately monetized at the expense of physicians and consumers. 

Taken together, these trends reveal the emergence of a new health data supply chain — one that spans both outside and inside the clinical setting. Patients are voluntarily sharing highly sensitive information with AI platforms on one end, while on the other, ambient systems are passively capturing clinical conversations in unprecedented detail. In both cases, patients often do not fully understand what is being collected, where it is stored, or who ultimately has access. What begins as a conversation is rapidly becoming a permanent, queryable record. 

This is not a failure of technology; it is a failure of policy. HIPAA was designed for a health care system built around hospitals and insurers, not conversational AI and always-on ambient computing. It does not account for systems that simulate medical dialogue or continuously capture clinical interactions. Nor does it address the growing convergence of clinical data, consumer data and financial analytics. Even when consent is obtained, it is often buried in lengthy disclosures that few patients read or fully comprehend. 

Americans are not careless with their health information. They are operating under assumptions that no longer hold true. They believe they are having private, protected conversations when, in reality, they are participating in a rapidly expanding data ecosystem that operates largely outside the traditional boundaries of medical confidentiality. As artificial intelligence becomes more embedded in both everyday life and clinical care, the gap between expectation and reality will only widen. 

The solution is not to retreat from innovation but to catch policy up with practice. That means rethinking how health data is defined, who is responsible for protecting it and how consent is obtained in an era where conversations themselves have become data. 

Until then, Americans will continue to speak freely — inside and outside the exam room — without fully understanding who else may be listening. 

Sreedhar Potarazu, MD, MBA, is an author, board-certified ophthalmologist and clinical director at American Eye Care.

data.title: America must not waste an opioid settlement lifeline
data.description:
For decades, the opioid crisis has devastated communities across the United States. More than 700,000 Americans have lost their lives to overdoses since the epidemic began, leaving families shattered and communities struggling to recover. Even as new data suggests that overdose deaths may finally be declining, the fight is far from over. Illicit fentanyl and a new emerging class of even more potent synthetic opioids remain among the most dangerous threats facing the country today.

In response to this crisis, pharmaceutical companies and distributors have agreed to roughly $57 billion in national opioid settlements that will flow to states and local governments over the coming years. These funds represent a rare opportunity: a chance to invest in treatment programs, strengthen prevention efforts, equip first responders, and expand recovery services in communities that desperately need help. But a troubling pattern is beginning to emerge.

A recently released report on government spending of opioid funds raises serious concerns about how some jurisdictions are using these resources. Instead of prioritizing addiction treatment, overdose prevention, and law-enforcement efforts to stop the flow of illicit narcotics, the report documents instances where funds are being directed toward projects only loosely related — or in some cases unrelated — to addressing opioid addiction.

These findings should alarm policymakers across the political spectrum.

Opioid settlements were never intended to become a revenue stream for state and local governments to relieve budget pressures or pay for unrelated initiatives. The purpose of these funds was clear: to confront the addiction crisis and prevent future overdose deaths. Diverting these dollars towards political pet projects or general spending not only violates the spirit of the settlements, it risks undermining the very progress communities are trying to make against addiction.

And the stakes could not be higher.

Today’s opioid crisis looks very different from the one that began two decades ago. Prescription opioid misuse has declined significantly in recent years, but synthetic drugs such as heroin and illicit fentanyl have filled the vacuum. Fentanyl is an extraordinarily potent synthetic opioid and increasingly present in counterfeit pills and other drugs sold on the street. Law enforcement officials warn that it now represents the deadliest drug threat the nation has ever encountered.

Addressing this evolving crisis requires real investment: expanding access to treatment for opioid-use disorder, distributing naloxone to reverse overdoses, strengthening prevention programs, and supporting law enforcement efforts aimed at disrupting trafficking networks. The opioid settlements were designed to provide precisely those resources. But if funds are siphoned away from those priorities, the opportunity they represent could quickly slip away.

During my time in Congress, including my service as chairman of the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, one lesson became clear: transparency is the most powerful tool for ensuring taxpayer dollars are spent as intended. That principle applies just as strongly in ensuring accountability with regards to the stewardship of opioid settlement funds as it does to federal programs.

Therefore, states should require detailed public reporting on how settlement funds are allocated and used. Public dashboards that track spending by program, location and outcome could allow citizens, journalists and policymakers alike to see where the money is going. Local governments receiving these funds should be required to submit detailed plans showing how their spending will directly reduce overdoses, expand treatment access or strengthen prevention efforts.

Several states have already taken steps in this direction by publishing public reports and establishing oversight mechanisms to ensure settlement dollars are used for legitimate opioid-abatement purposes, but this is unfortunately not a national standard. Those models demonstrate that transparency and accountability are not obstacles to innovation — they are safeguards that ensure the money truly reaches the communities and programs that need it most — and they should be replicated across the country.

The opioid crisis is one of the most devastating public health challenges our nation has ever faced and the American people deserve confidence that the billions secured through these legal settlements will be used to save lives and strengthen recovery. But that promise will only be fulfilled if governments at every level treat these funds with the seriousness and accountability that the crisis demands.

Dan Burton served as a member of Congress from Indiana from 1983-2013. He is the former chairman of the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

data.title: Live updates: Iran shoots down US F-15; White House's 2027 budget seeks $1.5T for Pentagon
data.description:
Iran shot down a U.S. fighter jet, with Iranian and U.S. media reporting a search and rescue for two service members is underway. It is the first jet downed in the five-week war.

The White House released its fiscal year 2027 budget proposal to Congress, seeking $1.5 trillion in defense spending, a roughly 40 percent increase from 2026 funding levels. A base of $1.1 trillion will be specifically for the Pentagon Department of Defense, while $350 billion will be requested through Congress’s budget reconciliation process for “critical Administration priorities” and Trump’s planned “Golden Dome” missile defense system. Find more details here.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth carried out another personnel overhaul on Thursday. forcing the retirement of Army Chief of Staff Gen. Randy George. The Pentagon, meanwhile, may be returning the USS Gerald R. Ford to the Middle East, as it sailed out of a five-day docking in Croatia on Thursday.

In Iran, Tehran is tightening its grip on the global oil supply chain amid rising gas prices by instituting a “toll booth” system on the Strait of Hormuz.

The ouster of Attorney General Pam Bondi on Thursday came as she didn’t secure convictions of President Trump’s enemies. It also put Todd Blanche, Trump’s former personal attorney, atop the Justice Department, for now. Here’s what to know about the shakeup.

The president on Friday has a series of White House meetings.

Follow live below.

data.title: Swalwell says Bondi didn't tip him off on report
data.description:
Rep. Eric Swalwell (D- Calif.) on Thursday denied a claim that Attorney General Pam Bondi was fired for leaking information to him about a decade-old investigation into his relationship with an alleged Chinese spy. 

“The attorney general did not tip us off. No one in her orbit tipped us off,” Swalwell said in an interview on MS NOW.

President Trump fired Bondi on Thursday after 14 months on the job, a decision that sources have linked to her handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files and to her lack of success in prosecuting some of Trump’s adversaries. Bondi had worked with Trump since defending him in cases challenging the 2020 election results.

Swalwell, a key figure in Trump’s first impeachment, is the leading Democratic candidate for governor in California’s open primary, with 13.4 percent support, according to Decision Desk HQ polling averages.

The primary is set for June 2, with the top two vote-getters, regardless of party, advancing to the general election. More than a half dozen Democratic candidates are on the primary ballot, and polls have shown voters in the heavily blue state spreading their support among them.

That has created the unusual possibility of two Republican candidates making it through to the November general election ballot if Swalwell or another Democratic candidate comes in third in June.

The Washington Post reported on Saturday that Patel has pushed FBI agents to gather documents on the relationship between Swalwell and Christina Fang, a suspected Chinese intelligence operative known as Fang Fang. She reportedly helped Swalwell with fundraising for his 2014 reelection campaign and was an intern in his congressional office.

The agents and other personnel were directed to gather and redact sensitive information from documents from the investigation, which has long been closed, in preparation for sharing with senior Trump administration officials, the Post reported.

Reports swirled Thursday that Trump was furious that Bondi had tipped off Swalwell about the plans to release the files.

Swalwell’s comments on MS NOW echoed a statement he issued on Thursday.

“We had no heads-up by anyone in the administration. None,” he said.

“These stories would be laughable if not so outrageous. An administration that is now at 33% approval is looking to blame anyone but the right people — themselves.”

Earlier this week, Swalwell said Trump would like a Republican ally to win the California governor’s race, effectively giving the president “a Western White House” in Sacramento.

“The way to do that is to knock out the leading candidate and try and get two Republicans through in our top two primary,” he said Monday in an interview on “CNN News Central.”

In September, Trump publicly urged Bondi to speed up prosecution of his political opponents, complaining that “nothing is being done.”

Swalwell told CNN that Trump “wants an attorney general that is willing to break the law” and is willing to “weaponize the Department of Justice against his political enemies.”

data.title: GOP rep on Hegseth firing Army chief of staff: 'I will look into it immediately'
data.description:
Rep. Rich McCormick (R-Ga.) on Thursday said he will “immediately” look into Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s recent decision to fire Army chief of staff Gen. Randy George.

“That would be very surprising to me,” McCormick told Newsmax’s Ed Henry, seemingly perplexed by the news. “I would look into it immediately because those are people I know very well.” 

Hegseth asked George, whose four-year term would not have been up until 2027, to step down and immediately retire, according to a Pentagon official who spoke to The Hill on Thursday.

McCormick, who sits on the House Armed Services Committee (HASC), called the former chief of staff a “brilliant mind” and said he’d be “very curious to know why” the Biden appointee had been fired. 

“I’ve never heard him say anything contrary to what the president’s trying to achieve,” the Georgia Republican said. “I thought he’s done a really good job getting the Army ready for war. So, I’d like to hear more because that’s concerning to me.” 

The ouster was another marker of a recent pattern of tension between Hegseth and U.S. Army leadership. Since the start of his term, Hegseth has removed more than a dozen senior military officers including, most recently, Col. David Butler, a top adviser to Army Secretary Dan Driscoll.

Alongside George, the defense secretary also removed Maj. Gen. William Green, the Army’s chief of chaplains, and Gen. David Hodne, who has led the Army’s Transformation and Training Command since last year, from their roles, according to the Pentagon official.

Lt. Gen. Christopher LaNeve, the Army’s current vice chief of staff, will serve as the acting chief of staff. 

A source familiar with the decision told CBS News, who first reported on George’s firing, that Hegseth wanted someone for the role who would implement his and Trump’s vision for the forces.

“We are grateful for his service, but it was time for a leadership change in the Army,” a senior Defense Department official also reportedly told CBS.

The firing comes amid the ongoing U.S. military operation in Iran. An American fighter jet was reportedly struck down by Tehran earlier on Friday. 

“Hegseth and Trump firing the highest ranking Army officer, in the middle of a war they started, shows you exactly where their priorities are,” Rep. Pat Ryan (D-N.Y.), who also sits on the HASC, said Thursday on the social platform X.
